 | c = Definition of $\prec_2$ 
 }} 
 {{end-eqn}}\nHence the result.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \preceq_1}$ be a [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]].
Let $\struct {T, \preceq_2}$ be an [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered set]].
Let $\phi: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Then $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Order Embedding|order embedding]] {{iff}} $\phi$ is [[Definition:Strictly Increasing Mapping|strictly increasing]].
That is: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x \preceq_1 y \iff \map \phi x \preceq_2 \map \phi y$ 
 {{iff}}: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x \prec_1 y \implies \map \phi x \prec_2 \map \phi y$
Every [[Definition:Finite Set|finite]] [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]] is [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S, \preceq}$ be a [[Definition:Finite Set|finite]] [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]].
From [[Condition for Well-Foundedness]], $\struct {S, \preceq}$ is [[Definition:Well-Founded|well-founded]] {{iff}} there is no [[Definition:Infinite Sequence|infinite sequence]] $\sequence {a_n}$ of [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $S$ such that $ orall n \in \N: a_{n + 1} \prec a_n$.
If it were the case that $\struct {S, \preceq}$ had such an [[Definition:Infinite Sequence|infinite sequence]], then at least some of the [[Definition:Term of Sequence|terms]] would be repeated in that sequence.
So there would be, for example: 
 :$s_i \preceq s_j \preceq s_k \preceq s_i$ 
 and $\preceq$ would therefore not be [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]] and so not a [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]].
So $\struct {S, \preceq}$ is [[Definition:Well-Founded|well-founded]].
The result follows from the definition of [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered set]].
{{qed}}

problem: Every [[Definition:Non-Empty Set|non-empty]] [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of a [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered set]] is itself [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S, \preceq}$ be a [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered set]].
Let $T \subseteq S$.
Let $X \subseteq T$.
By [[Subset Relation is Transitive]], $X \subseteq S$.
By the definition of a [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered set]], $X$ has a [[Definition:Smallest Element|smallest element]].
It follows by definition that $T$ is [[Definition:Well-Ordered Set|well-ordered]].
Hence the result. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: Let $(S, \circ)$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].\nLet $x, y, z \in S$.\nIf $x$ [[Definition:Commute|commutes]] with both $y$ and $z$, then $x$ [[Definition:Commute|commutes]] with $y \circ z$.

Let $\circ$ be a [[Definition:Binary Operation|binary operation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nLet $\circ$ be [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].\nThen $\circ$ is [[Definition:Anticommutative|anticommutative]] {{iff}}: 
 : $(1): \quad \circ$ is [[Definition:Idempotent Operation|idempotent]] 
 and: 
 : $(2): \quad \forall a, b \in S: a \circ b \circ a = a$.
solution_steps:
Let $\circ$ be an [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative operation]] on $S$.
=== Necessary Condition ===
Suppose $\circ$ is [[Definition:Anticommutative|anticommutative]].
Let $a \circ a = x$ for some $a \in S$.

problem:
Let $\circ$ be a [[Definition:Binary Operation|binary operation]] on a set $S$.\nLet $\circ$ be both [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] and [[Definition:Anticommutative|anticommutative]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall x, y, z \in S: x \circ y \circ z = x \circ z$

Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].\nLet $x \sqbrk c y = c$ be a [[Definition:Constant Operation|constant operation]] on $S$.\nThen $\sqbrk c$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operation]]: 
 :$\forall x, y \in S: x \sqbrk c y = y \sqbrk c x$

Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].\nLet $x \left[{c}\right] y = c$ be a [[Definition:Constant Operation|constant operation]] on $S$.\nThen $\left[{c}\right]$ is an [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative operation]]: 
 :$\forall x, y, z \in S: \left({x \left[{c}\right] y}\right) \left[{c}\right] z = x \left[{c}\right] \left({y \left[{c}\right] z}\right)$

The [[Definition:Left Operation|left operation]] is [[Definition:Idempotent Operation|idempotent]]: 
 :$\forall x: x \leftarrow x = x$
solution_steps:
Immediate from the definition of the [[Definition:Left Operation|left operation]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
The [[Definition:Max Operation|Max]] and [[Definition:Min Operation|Min]] operations are [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]]: 
 : $\max \left({x, y}\right) = \max \left({y, x}\right)$ 
 : $\min \left({x, y}\right) = \min \left({y, x}\right)$

The [[Definition:Max Operation|Max]] and [[Definition:Min Operation|Min]] operations are [[Definition:Idempotent Operation|idempotent]]: 
 : $\max \left({x, x}\right) = x$ 
 : $\min \left({x, x}\right) = x$
solution_steps:
Follows immediately from the definitions of [[Definition:Max Operation|Max]] and [[Definition:Min Operation|Min]]. 
 {{Qed}} 
 [[Category:Max and Min Operations]] 
 [[Category:Idempotence]]

problem:
The [[Definition:Max Operation|Max]] and [[Definition:Min Operation|Min]] operations are [[Definition:Distributive Operation|distributive]] over each other: 
 :$\max \set {x, \min \set {y, z} } = \min \set {\max \set {x, y}, \max \set {x, z} }$ 
 :$\max \set {\min \set {x, y}, z} = \min \set {\max \set {x, z}, \max \set {y, z} }$ 
 :$\min \set {x, \max \set {y, z} } = \max \set {\min \set {x, y}, \min \set {x, z} }$ 
 :$\min \set {\max \set {x, y}, z} = \max \set {\min \set {x, z}, \min \set {y, z} }$

Two [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\struct {S, \circ}$ and $\struct {T, *}$ are [[Definition:Equality|equal]] {{iff}}: 
 :$S = T$ 
 :$\forall a, b \in S: a \circ b = a * b$
solution_steps:
This follows from [[Definition:Set Equality|set equality]] and [[Equality of Ordered Pairs]].

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
Let $T \subseteq S$.
Let $T$ be [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ$.
Then $\struct {T, \circ {estriction_T} }$ is also a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]], where $\circ {estriction_T}$ is the [[Definition:Restriction of Operation|restriction]] of $\circ$ to $T$.

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $T \subseteq S$.
Let the [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\circ$ be [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]] on $\struct {S, \circ}$.
Then the [[Definition:Restriction of Operation|restriction]] $\circ {estriction_T}$ of $\circ$ to $T$ is also [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Magma|magma]].\nLet $x \in S$ be an [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]] element of $\struct {S, \circ}$.\nThen $\struct {\set x, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Submagma|submagma]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
solution_steps:
By [[Singleton of Element is Subset]]: 
 : $x \in S \iff \set x \subseteq S$
By the definition of [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotence]]: 
 : $x \circ x = x \in \set x$
Thus $\set x$ is a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of $S$ which is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ$.
By the definition of [[Definition:Submagma|submagma]], the result follows.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Magma|magma]].\nLet $T \subseteq S$.\nThen $\left({T, \circ}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Magma|magma]] {{iff}} $T \circ T \subseteq T$, where $T \circ T$ is the [[Definition:Subset Product|subset product]] of $T$ with itself.
solution_steps:
By definition: 
 : $T \circ T = \left\{{x = a \circ b: a, b \in T}ight\}$
=== Necessary Condition ===
Let $\left({T, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Magma|magma]].
Then $T$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
That is: 
 :$ orall x, y \in T: x \circ y \in T$
Thus: 
 : $x \circ y \in T \circ T \implies x \circ y \in T$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Let $T \circ T \subseteq T$.
Then: 
 : $x \circ y \in T \circ T \implies x \circ y \in T$
That is, $T$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
Therefore $\left({T, \circ}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Magma|magma]] by definition.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].\nThen the [[Definition:Operation Induced on Power Set|operation $\circ_\PP$ induced]] on the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$ is also [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Magma|magma]].\nIf $\circ$ is [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]], then the [[Definition:Operation Induced on Power Set|operation $\circ_\mathcal P$ induced]] on the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$ is also [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Magma|magma]].\nLet $\powerset S$ be the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$.\nLet $X, Y, Z \in \powerset S$.\nThen: 
 :$X \subseteq Y \implies \paren {X \circ Z} \subseteq \paren {Y \circ Z}$ 
 :$X \subseteq Y \implies \paren {Z \circ X} \subseteq \paren {Z \circ Y}$ 
 where $X \circ Z$ etc. denotes [[Definition:Subset Product|subset product]].
solution_steps:
Let $x \in X, z \in Z$.\nThen: 
 :$x \circ z \in X \circ Z$ and $z \circ x \in Z \circ X$\nNow: 
 :$Y \circ Z = \set {y \circ z: y \in Y, z \in Z}$ 
 :$Z \circ Y = \set {z \circ y: y \in Y, z \in Z}$\nBut by the definition of a [[Definition:Subset|subset]]: 
 :$x \in X \implies x \in Y$\nThus: 
 :$x \circ z \in Y \circ Z$ and $z \circ x \in Z \circ Y$ 
 and the result follows. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\left ({S, \circ}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $\left ({T, \circ}ight) \subseteq \left ({S, \circ}ight)$.
Let $x \in T$ be [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] in $S$.
Then $x$ is also [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] in $T$.
solution_steps:
Let $x \in T$ be [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] in $S$.
Then by definition, $x \in T$ is [[Definition:Left Cancellable Element|left cancellable]] in $S$.
It follows from [[Left Cancellable Element is Left Cancellable in Subset]] that $x \in T$ is [[Definition:Left Cancellable Element|left cancellable]] in $T$.
Again by definition, $x \in T$ is [[Definition:Right Cancellable Element|right cancellable]] in $S$.
It follows from [[Right Cancellable Element is Right Cancellable in Subset]] that $x \in T$ is [[Definition:Right Cancellable Element|right cancellable]] in $T$.
Thus $x$ is also both [[Definition:Left Cancellable Element|left cancellable]] and [[Definition:Right Cancellable Element|right cancellable]] in $T$.
So by definition, $x$ is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] in $T$.
{{qed}}

problem:
To show that an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\left({T, \circ}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] $\struct {S, \circ}$, we need to show only that: 
 : $(1): \quad T \subseteq S$ 
 : $(2): \quad \circ$ is a [[Definition:Closed Operation|closed operation]] in $T$.
solution_steps:
From [[Restriction of Associative Operation is Associative]], if $\circ$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] on $\struct {S, \circ}$, then it will also be [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] on $\struct {T, \circ}$.\nThus we do not need to check for [[Definition:Associative Operation|associativity]] in $\struct {T, \circ}$, as that has been inherited from its [[Definition:Extension of Operation|extension]] $\struct {S, \circ}$.\nSo, once we have established that $T \subseteq S$, all we need to do is to check for $\circ$ to be a [[Definition:Closed Operation|closed operation]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].\nThen $a \in S$ is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] {{iff}}: 
 ::the [[Definition:Left Regular Representation|left regular representation]] $\map {\lambda_a} x$ is [[Definition:Injection|injective]] 
 and 
 ::the [[Definition:Right Regular Representation|right regular representation]] $\map {\rho_a} x$ is [[Definition:Injection|injective]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].\nIf $\struct {S, \circ}$ has more than one [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]], then it has no [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] with more than one [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]].
Take any two of these, and call them $e_{L_1}$ and $e_{L_2}$, where $e_{L_1} 
e e_{L_2}$.
Suppose $\struct {S, \circ}$ has a [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].
Call this [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]] $e_R$.
Then, by the behaviour of $e_R$, $e_{L_1}$ and $e_{L_2}$: 
 : $e_{L_1} = e_{L_1} \circ e_R = e_R$ 
 : $e_{L_2} = e_{L_2} \circ e_R = e_R$
So $e_{L_1} = e_R = e_{L_2}$, which contradicts the supposition that $e_{L_1}$ and $e_{L_2}$ are different.
Therefore, in an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] with more than one [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]], there can be no [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {S, \leftarrow}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] in which the [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\leftarrow$ is the [[Definition:Left Operation|left operation]].\nThen no matter what $S$ is, $\struct {S, \leftarrow}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] all of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Right Identity|right identities]].\nThus it can be seen that any [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]] in a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] is not necessarily [[Definition:Unique|unique]].
solution_steps:
From [[Left Operation is Associative]], $\leftarrow$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
By the nature of the [[Definition:Left Operation|left operation]], $\struct {S, \leftarrow}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]]: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x \leftarrow y = x \in S$ 
 whatever $S$ may be.
So $\struct {S, \leftarrow}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
From the definition of [[Definition:Left Operation|left operation]]: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x \leftarrow y = x$ 
 from which it is apparent that all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $S$ are [[Definition:Right Identity|right identities]].
From [[More than one Right Identity then no Left Identity]], it also follows that there is no [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {S, \rightarrow}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] in which the [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\rightarrow$ is the [[Definition:Right Operation|right operation]].\nThen $\struct {S, \rightarrow}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] all of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Left Identity|left identities]].\nThus it can be seen that any [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]] in a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] is not necessarily [[Definition:Unique|unique]].
solution_steps:
From [[Right Operation is Associative]], $ightarrow$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
By the nature of the [[Definition:Right Operation|right operation]], $\struct {S, ightarrow}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]]: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x ightarrow y = y \in S$ 
 whatever $S$ may be.
So $\struct {S, ightarrow}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
From the definition of [[Definition:Right Operation|right operation]]: 
 :$ orall x, y \in S: x ightarrow y = y$ 
 from which it is apparent that all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $S$ are [[Definition:Left Identity|left identities]].
{{qed}}
From [[More than one Left Identity then no Right Identity]], it also follows that there is no [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $e_L \in S$ be a [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]], and $e_R \in S$ be a [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].
Then: 
 :$e_L = e_R$ 
 that is, both the [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]] and [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]] are the same, and are therefore an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e$.
Furthermore, $e$ is the only [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]] and [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]] for $\circ$.
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] such that: 
 :$\exists e_L \in S: \forall x \in S: e_L \circ x = x$ 
 :$\exists e_R \in S: \forall x \in S: x \circ e_R = x$\nThen $e_L = e_L \circ e_R = e_R$ by both the above, hence the result.\nThe uniqueness of the left and right identity is a direct result of [[Identity is Unique]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].\nLet $s \in S$ be such that: 
 :$\forall a \in S: \exists x, y \in S: s \circ x = a = y \circ s$\nThen $\struct {S, \circ}$ has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]].
The [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
solution_steps:
Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e$.\nLet $x, y \in S$ such that $x \circ e = y \circ e$\nThen, by the definition of the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]]: 
 :$x = x \circ e = y \circ e = y$ 
 ... thus $x = y$ and the result is proved.\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $e_S$ is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\left({S, \circ}\right)$.\nThen $e_S$ is the only [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable element]] of $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ that is [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]].

Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $S^S$ be the [[Definition:Set of All Mappings|set of all mappings]] from $S$ to itself.
Let the [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\circ$ represent [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composition of mappings]].
Then the [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\struct {S^S, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] is the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $S$.
solution_steps:
By [[Set of All Self-Maps is Semigroup]], $\struct {S^S, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
By [[Identity Mapping is Left Identity]] and [[Identity Mapping is Right Identity]] the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $S$ is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] of $\struct {S^S, \circ}$.
Since $\struct {S^S, \circ}$ is a semigroup with an identity element, it is a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] by definition.
{{qed}}

problem:
To show that $\left({T, \circ}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Submonoid|submonoid]] of a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] $\left({S, \circ}\right)$, we need to show that: 
 :$(1): \quad T \subseteq S$ 
 :$(2): \quad \left({T, \circ}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Magma|magma]] (i.e. that it is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]]) 
 :$(3): \quad \left({T, \circ}\right)$ has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]], all of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].\nLet $\struct {T, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Submonoid|submonoid]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.\nThen the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\struct {T, \circ}$ is the same [[Definition:Element|element]] as the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
solution_steps:
By [[Identity of Monoid is Unique]]: 
 : there is only one [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ 
 and: 
 : there is only one [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] of $\struct {T, \circ}$.
Let $e_S$ be the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$, and $e_T$ the identity of $\struct {T, \circ}$.
From [[Identity is only Idempotent Cancellable Element]], $e_S$ is the only [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable element]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ which is [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]].
But all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Thus $e_S$ is the only [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent element]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
Again, all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Thus from [[Cancellable Element is Cancellable in Subset]], all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $\struct {T, \circ}$ are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Thus, $e_T$ is the only [[Definition:Element|element]] of $\struct {T, \circ}$ which is [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]].
Thus, as $e_T \in S$, we have $e_S \circ e_T = e_T = e_T \circ e_T$ and thus $e_S = e_T$.
{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] $\struct {S, \circ}$ form a [[Definition:Submonoid|submonoid]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
solution_steps:
Let $C$ be the [[Definition:Set|set]] of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
Obviously $C \subseteq S$.
From [[Cancellable Elements of Semigroup form Subsemigroup]], $\struct {C, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of $S$.
Let $e_S$ be the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
From [[Identity of Monoid is Cancellable]], $e_S$ is cancellable, therefore $e_S \in C$.
As $e_S$ is the identity of $S$, we have that: 
 :$ orall x \in C: x \circ e_S = x = e_S \circ x$.
Thus $e_S$ is the identity of $\struct {C, \circ}$, which is therefore a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]].
As $C \subseteq S$, the result follows.
{{qed}} 
 [[Category:Monoids]]

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] with a [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]] $e_L$.
Let $x \in S$ such that $\exists x_L: x_L \circ x = e_L$, that is $x$ has a [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left inverse]] with respect to the [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]].
Then: 
 :$\paren {x \circ x_L} \circ \paren {x \circ x_L} = x \circ x_L$
That is, $x \circ x_L$ is [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] with a [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]] $e_R$.
Let $x \in S$ such that $\exists x_R: x \circ x_R = e_R$, i.e. $x$ has a [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right inverse]] with respect to the [[Definition:Right Identity|right identity]].
Then: 
 :$\paren {x_R \circ x} \circ \paren {x_R \circ x} = x_R \circ x$
That is, $x_R \circ x$ is [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] with a [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]] $e_L$ such that: 
 :$\forall x \in S: \exists x_L: x_L \circ x = e_L$\nThat is, every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ has a [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left inverse]] with respect to the [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]].\nThen $x \circ x_L = e_L$, that is, $x_L$ is also a [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right inverse]] with respect to the [[Definition:Left Identity|left identity]].
solution_steps: Let $y = x \circ x_L$.

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] with [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $e_S$.
Let $x \in S$ such that $x$ has both a [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left inverse]] and a [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right inverse]]. That is: 
 :$\exists x_L \in S: x_L \circ x = e_S$ 
 :$\exists x_R \in S: x \circ x_R = e_S$
Then $x_L = x_R$, that is, $x$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]].
Furthermore, that element is the ''only'' [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] (both [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right]] and [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left]]) for $x$

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e_S$.
Let $x, y \in S$.
Let: 
 :$(1): \quad x \circ y$ have a [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left inverse]] for $\circ$ 
 :$(2): \quad y \circ x$ have a [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right inverse]] for $\circ$.
Then both $x$ and $y$ are [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] for $\circ$.
solution_steps: Let $z_L$ be the [[Definition:Left Inverse Element|left inverse]] of $x \circ y$ and $z_R$ be the [[Definition:Right Inverse Element|right inverse]] of $y \circ x$.

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] with an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $e$.
Let the [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $e$ be $e^{-1}$.
Then: 
 : $e^{-1} = e$
That is, $e$ is [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]].
solution_steps:
From [[Identity Element is Idempotent]]: 
 :$e \circ e = e$\nHence the result by definition of [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]].\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] where $\circ$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].\nLet $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $e_S$.\nAn [[Definition:Element|element]] of $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ which is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] is also [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]].\nLet $a \in S$ be [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]].\nThen the [[Definition:Left Regular Representation|left regular representation]] $\lambda_a$ and the [[Definition:Right Regular Representation|right regular representation]] $\rho_a$ are [[Definition:Permutation|permutations]] of $S$.
A [[Definition:Group|group]] is an [[Definition:Inverse Semigroup|inverse semigroup]] with an [[Definition:Identity (Abstract Algebra)|identity]].
solution_steps: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e_S$.
Let $C$ be the [[Definition:Set|set]] of all [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $S$.
Let $T$ be the [[Definition:Set|set]] of all [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible elements]] of $S$.
Then $\struct {T, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $\struct {C, \circ}$.
solution_steps:
From [[Cancellable Elements of Monoid form Submonoid]], $\struct {C, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Submonoid|submonoid]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
Let its [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] be $e_C$ (which may or may not be the same as $e_S$).
Let $T$ be the [[Definition:Set|set]] of all [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible elements]] of $S$.
From [[Invertible Element of Monoid is Cancellable]], all the [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible elements]] of $S$ are also all [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]], so $T \subseteq C$.
Let $x, y \in T$.
Clearly $x^{-1}, y^{-1} \in T$, as if $x, y$ are [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]], then so are their [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverses]].
Taking the [[Definition:Group Axioms|group axioms]] in turn:
=== G0: Closure ===
By [[Inverse of Product]], $x^{-1} \circ y^{-1} \in T$.
Thus $\struct {T, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== G1: Associativity ===
This is inherited from $S$, by [[Subsemigroup Closure Test]].
Thus $\struct {T, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== G2: Identity ===
All the elements of $\struct {C, \circ}$ are by definition [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]]
So, by [[Identity of Cancellable Monoid is Identity of Submonoid]]: 
 : $e_C \in T$
Thus $\struct {T, \circ}$ has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== G3: Inverses ===
By [[Inverse of Inverse in Monoid]]: 
 :$\struct {x^{-1} \circ y^{-1} }^{-1} \in T$
Thus every element of $\struct {T, \circ}$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]].
{{qed|lemma}}
All the [[Definition:Group Axioms|group axioms]] are thus seen to be fulfilled, and so $\struct {T, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {T, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]], and let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$ be the [[Definition:Induced Structure|structure on $T^S$ induced]] by $\circ$.
Let $\circ$ be [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
Then the [[Definition:Pointwise Operation|pointwise operation $\oplus$ induced]] on $T^S$ by $\circ$ is also [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].

Let $\struct {T, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]], and let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$ be the [[Definition:Induced Structure|structure on $T^S$ induced]] by $\circ$.
Let $\circ$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operation]].
Then the [[Definition:Pointwise Operation|pointwise operation $\oplus$ induced]] on $T^S$ by $\circ$ is also [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]].

Let $\struct {T, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]], and let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$ be the [[Definition:Induced Structure|structure on $T^S$ induced]] by $\circ$.
Let $e$ be an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\circ$.
Then the [[Definition:Constant Mapping|constant mapping]] $f_e: S 	o T$ defined as: 
 :$ orall x \in S: \map {f_e} x = e$ 
 is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for the [[Definition:Pointwise Operation|pointwise operation $\oplus$ induced]] on $T^S$ by $\circ$.

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e$.
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $\struct {G^S, \oplus}$ be the [[Definition:Induced Structure|structure on $G^S$ induced]] by $\circ$.
Then $\struct {G^S, \oplus}$ is an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
solution_steps:
From [[Structure Induced by Group Operation is Group]], $\struct {G^S, \oplus}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nFrom [[Structure Induced by Commutative Operation is Commutative]], so is the [[Definition:Pointwise Operation|pointwise operation it induces]] on $G^S$.\nHence the result.\n{{qed}} 
 [[Category:Abelian Groups]] 
 [[Category:Pointwise Operations]]

problem:
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]] and let $\powerset S$ be its [[Definition:Power Set|power set]].
Then $\struct {\powerset S, \cup}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $\O$.
The only [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible element]] of this [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|structure]] is $\O$.
Thus (except in the [[Definition:Degenerate Case|degenerate case]] $S = \O$) $\struct {\powerset S, \cup}$ cannot be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
solution_steps:
From [[Power Set is Closed under Union]]: 
 :$ orall A, B \in \powerset S: A \cup B \in \powerset S$
From [[Set System Closed under Union is Commutative Semigroup]], $\struct {\powerset S, \cup}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]].
From [[Identity of Power Set with Union]], $\O$ acts as the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\struct {\powerset S, \cup}$.
It remains to be shown that only $\O$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]]:
For $T \subseteq S$ to have an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] under $\cup$, we require $T^{-1} \cup T = \O$.
From this it follows that $T = \O = T^{-1}$.
The result follows by definition of [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]] and let $\powerset S$ be its [[Definition:Power Set|power set]].
Then $\struct {\powerset S, \cap}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $S$.
The only [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible element]] of this [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|structure]] is $S$.
Thus (except in the [[Definition:Degenerate Case|degenerate case]] $S = \O$) $\struct {\powerset S, \cap}$ cannot be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
solution_steps:
From [[Power Set is Closed under Intersection]]: 
 :$ orall A, B \in \powerset S: A \cap B \in \powerset S$
From [[Set System Closed under Intersection is Commutative Semigroup]], $\struct {\powerset S, \cap}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]].
From [[Identity of Power Set with Intersection]], we have that $S$ acts as the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]].
It remains to be shown that only $S$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]]:
For $T \subseteq S$ to have an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] under $\cap$, we require $T^{-1} \cap T = S$.
From this it follows that $T = S = T^{-1}$.
The result follows by definition of [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]].
{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]] $\Delta_S$ on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ is [[Definition:Universally Compatible Relation|universally compatible]] with every [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] on $S$.
solution_steps: {{questionable|It needs to be pointed out that the definition of [[Definition:Universally Compatible Relation|universally compatible relation]] may not be correct as such.

problem: The [[Definition:Trivial Relation|trivial relation]] $\mathcal R = S \times S$ on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ is [[Definition:Universally Congruent|universally congruent]] with every [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed operation]] on $S$.
solution_steps: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be any [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] which is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] for $\circ$.\nBy definition of [[Definition:Trivial Relation|trivial relation]], $x \in S \land y \in S \implies x \mathcal R y$.

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\struct {S 	imes T, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$.
Let $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$ be [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
Then $\struct {S 	imes T, \circ}$ is also [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].

Let $\struct {S \times T, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of the two [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$.\nLet $\circ_1$ and $\circ_2$ be [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].\nThen $\circ$ is also [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
The [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of two [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroups]] is itself a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
solution_steps: * From [[External Direct Product Closure]], the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of two closed [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] is [[Definition:Closure (Abstract Algebra)|closed]].

* From [[External Direct Product Associativity]], the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of two [[Definition:Associative Algebraic Structure|associative algebraic structures]] is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].\nThe result follows.\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S \times T, \circ}\right)$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of the two [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\left({S, \circ_1}\right)$ and $\left({T, \circ_2}\right)$.\nLet $\circ_1$ and $\circ_2$ be [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operations]].\nThen $\circ$ is also a [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operation]].

Let $\struct {S \times T, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of the two [[Definition:Monoid|monoids]] $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$.\nLet: 
 : $e_S$ be the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ 
 and: 
 : $e_T$ be the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\struct {T, \circ_2}$.\nThen $\tuple {e_S, e_T}$ is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\struct {S \times T, \circ}$.

Let $\struct {S \times T, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of the two [[Definition:Monoid|monoids]] $\struct {S, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {T, \circ_2}$.\nLet: 
 :$s^{-1}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $s \in \struct {S, \circ_1}$ 
 and: 
 :$t^{-1}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $t \in \struct {T, \circ_2}$.\nThen $\tuple {s^{-1}, t^{-1} }$ is an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $\tuple {s, t} \in \struct {S \times T, \circ}$.

Let $\phi: \left({S, \circ_1, \circ_2, \ldots, \circ_n}\right) \to \left({T, *_1, *_2, \ldots, *_n}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] from one [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\left({S, \circ_1, \circ_2, \ldots, \circ_n}\right)$ to another $\left({T, *_1, *_2, \ldots, *_n}\right)$.\nLet $\circ_k$ have the [[Definition:Morphism Property|morphism property]] under $\phi$ for some operation $\circ_k$ in $\left({S, \circ_1, \circ_2, \ldots, \circ_n}\right)$.\nThen the following properties hold: 
 : If $S' \subseteq S$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ_k$, then $\phi \left[{S'}\right]$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $*_k$ 
 : If $T' \subseteq T$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $*_k$, then $\phi^{-1} \left[{T'}\right]$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ_k$ 
 where $\phi \left[{S'}\right]$ denotes the [[Definition:Image of Subset under Mapping|image of $S'$]].

Let:

 be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] from one [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]]:

problem:
Let $\mathcal R$ be a [[Definition:Congruence Relation|congruence relation]] on an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\struct {S, \circ}$.\nThen the [[Definition:Quotient Mapping|quotient mapping]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ to the [[Definition:Quotient Structure|quotient structure]] $\struct {S / \mathcal R, \circ_\mathcal R}$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]]: 
 :$q_\mathcal R: \struct {S, \circ} \to \struct {S / \mathcal R, \circ_\mathcal R}: \forall x, y \in S: \map {q_\mathcal R} {x \circ y} = \map {q_\mathcal R} x \circ_\mathcal R \map {q_\mathcal R} y$\nThis is sometimes called the '''canonical epimorphism''' from $\struct {S, \circ}$ to $\struct {S / \mathcal R, \circ_\mathcal R}$.

Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].\nLet $\phi: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].\nThen a [[Definition:Surjective Restriction|surjective restriction]] of $\phi$ can be produced by limiting the [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] of $\phi$ to its [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] $\operatorname{Im} \left({\phi}\right)$.
solution_steps:
Let $\phi: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $\operatorname{Im} \left({\phi}ight) = T'$
By [[Morphism Property Preserves Closure]], $T'$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
From [[Restriction of Mapping to Image is Surjection]], $\phi 	o \operatorname{Im} \left({\phi}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Thus $\phi: S 	o T$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Therefore, by suitably restricting the codomain of a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]], it is possible to regard it as an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ and $\struct {T, *}$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \struct {S, \circ} 	o \struct {T, *}$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\circ$ be an [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative operation]].
Then $*$ is also an [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative operation]].

Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ and $\left({T, *}ight)$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \left({S, \circ}ight) 	o \left({T, *}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
Then $\left({T, *}ight)$ is also a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
solution_steps:
As $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]], then by definition it is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
As $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]], it is by definition [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]].
That is: 
 :$T = \phi \left[{S}ight]$ 
 where $\phi \left[{S}ight]$ denotes the [[Definition:Image of Subset under Mapping|image of $S$ under $\phi$]].
From [[Morphism Property Preserves Closure]] it follows that $\left({T, *}ight)$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
As $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]], then by definition $\circ$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
From [[Epimorphism Preserves Associativity]], $*$ is therefore also [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
So: 
 : $\left({T, *}ight)$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] 
 and: 
 : $*$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
Therefore, by definition, $\left({T, *}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ and $\struct {T, *}$ be [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroups]].
Let $\phi: \struct {S, \circ} 	o \struct {T, *}$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $S'$ be a [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of $S$.
Then $\phi \paren {S'}$ is a [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of $T$.
solution_steps:
By [[Restriction of Homomorphism to Image is Epimorphism]], $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]] onto its [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] .\nThen by [[Epimorphism Preserves Semigroups]], it follows that the [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] of $S'$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].\nThe result follows. 
 {{Qed}}
