problem:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ and $\left({T, *}ight)$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \left({S, \circ}ight) 	o \left({T, *}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\circ$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operation]].
Then $*$ is also a [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative operation]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ and $\struct {T, *}$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \struct {S, \circ} 	o \struct {T, *}$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $e_S$.
Then $\struct {T, *}$ has the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $\map \phi {e_S}$.

Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ and $\left({T, *}ight)$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \left({S, \circ}ight) 	o \left({T, *}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_S$.
Let $x^{-1}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse element]] of $x$ for $\circ$.
Then $\phi \left({x^{-1}}ight)$ is an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse element]] of $\phi \left({x}ight)$ for $*$.
That is: 
 : $\phi \left({x^{-1}}ight) = \left({\phi \left({x}ight)}ight)^{-1}$
solution_steps: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] in which $\circ$ has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]] $e_S$.\nFrom [[Epimorphism Preserves Identity]], it follows that $\left({T, *}\right)$ also has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]], which is $\phi \left({e_S}\right)$.\nLet $y$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $x$ in $\left({S, \circ}\right)$.

problem:
Let $\struct{S, \circ}$ and $\struct{T, *}$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \struct{S, \circ} 	o \struct{T, *}$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $\struct{S, \circ}$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_S$.
Let $\struct{T, *}$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_T$.
Let every element of $\struct{T, *}$ be [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Then $\map \phi {e_S}$ is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_T$.

Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ and $\left({T, *}ight)$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \left({S, \circ}ight) 	o \left({T, *}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_S$.
Let $\left({T, *}ight)$ also have an identity $e_T = \phi \left({e_S}ight)$.
If $x^{-1}$ is an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $x$ for $\circ$, then $\phi \left({x^{-1}}ight)$ is an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $\phi \left({x}ight)$ for $*$.
That is: 
 : $\phi \left({x^{-1}}ight) = \left({\phi \left({x}ight)}ight)^{-1}$

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $\struct {T, *}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
Let $\phi: \struct {S, \circ} 	o \struct {T, *}$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] $e_S$.
Then: 
 :$\map \phi {e_S} = e_T$
solution_steps:
By hypothesis, $\struct {T, *}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nBy the [[Cancellation Laws]], all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $T$ are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].\nThus [[Homomorphism with Cancellable Codomain Preserves Identity]] can be applied. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let: 
 : $\left({S_1 \times S_2, \circ}\right)$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of two [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\left({S_1, \circ_1}\right)$ and $\left({S_2, \circ_2}\right)$ 
 : $\left({T_1 \times T_2, *}\right)$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of two [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\left({T_1, *_1}\right)$ and $\left({T_2, *_2}\right)$ 
 : $\phi_1$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\left({S_1, \circ_1}\right)$ onto $\left({T_1, *_1}\right)$ 
 : $\phi_2$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\left({S_2, \circ_2}\right)$ onto $\left({T_2, *_2}\right)$.\nThen the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $\phi_1 \times \phi_2: \left({S_1 \times S_2, \circ}\right) \to \left({T_1 \times T_2, *}\right)$ defined as: 
 :$\left({\phi_1 \times \phi_2}\right) \left({\left({x, y}\right)}\right) = \left({\phi_1 \left({x}\right), \phi_2 \left({y}\right)}\right)$ 
 is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\left({S_1 \times S_2, \circ}\right)$ to $\left({T_1 \times T_2, *}\right)$.

The [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] of a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]] is [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphic]] to its [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]].\nThat is, if $\phi: S_1 \to S_2$ is a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]], then: 
 :$\phi: S_1 \to \Img \phi$ 
 is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S_1, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {S_2, \circ_2}$ be [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi$ be a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]] from $\struct {S_1, \circ_1}$ to $\struct {S_2, \circ_2}$.
Let $T = \Img \phi$ be the [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] of $\phi$.
By [[Morphism Property Preserves Closure]], $\struct {T, \circ_2}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
As $\phi$ is a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]], it is by definition an [[Definition:Injection|injective]] [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
From [[Restriction of Injection is Injection]], $\phi: S_1 	o \Img \phi$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
From [[Restriction of Mapping to Image is Surjection]], $\phi: S_1 	o \Img \phi$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Thus $\phi 	o \Img \phi$ is by definition a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
Thus $\phi: S_1 	o \Img \phi$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijective]] [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Hence, by definition, $\phi: S_1 	o \Img \phi$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ and $\left({T, *}\right)$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].\nLet $\phi: \left({S, \circ}\right) \to \left({T, *}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] iff $\phi^{-1}: \left({T, *}\right) \to \left({S, \circ}\right)$ is also an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].

[[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|Isomorphism]] is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] of [[Definition:Magma|magmas]].\nThis result applies to all [[Definition:Magma|magmas]]: [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|rings]], [[Definition:Group|groups]], [[Definition:R-Algebraic Structure|R-algebraic structures]] etc.
solution_steps:
To prove a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]], we need to prove it is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
So, checking in turn each of the criteria for [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]]:
=== Reflexive ===
From [[Identity Mapping is Automorphism]], the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S 	o S$ is an [[Definition:Automorphism (Abstract Algebra)|automorphism]], which is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] from a [[Definition:Magma|magma]] onto itself.
So $S \cong S$, and [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] is seen to be [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]]. 
 {{Qed|lemma}}
=== Symmetric ===
If $S \cong T$, then $\exists \phi: S 	o T$ such that $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].
Thus, from [[Inverse of Algebraic Structure Isomorphism is Isomorphism]], $\phi^{-1}: T 	o S$ is also an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]], and $T \cong S$.
Thus we have shown that $S \cong T \implies T \cong S$, so $\cong$ is [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]]. 
 {{Qed|lemma}}
=== Transitive ===
Let $S_1 \cong S_2$, and $S_2 \cong S_3$.
We can define these two [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphisms]]: 
 : $\phi: S_1 	o S_2$ 
 : $\psi: S_2 	o S_3$
From [[Composite of Isomorphisms in Algebraic Structure is Isomorphism]], we have that $\psi \circ \phi$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] between $S_1$ and $S_3$.
Thus we have shown that $\cong$ is [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]]. 
 {{Qed|lemma}}
Thus [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]], and therefore an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:External Direct Product|external direct product]] of the [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]] $\struct {S_1, \circ_1}$ and $\struct {S_2, \circ_2}$.\nThen: 
 :$\pr_1$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ to $\struct {S_1, \circ_1}$ 
 :$\pr_2$ is an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ to $\struct {S_2, \circ_2}$ 
 where $\pr_1$ and $\pr_2$ are the [[Definition:First Projection|first]] and [[Definition:Second Projection|second projection]] respectively of $\struct {S, \circ}$.

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ and $\struct {T, *}$ be [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structures]].
Let $\phi: \struct {S, \circ} 	o \struct {T, *}$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].
Let $\mathcal R_\phi$ be the [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|equivalence induced by $\phi$]].
Let $S / \mathcal R_\phi$ be the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient of $S$ by $\mathcal R_\phi$]].
Let $q_{\mathcal R_\phi}: S 	o S / \mathcal R_\phi$ be the [[Definition:Quotient Mapping|quotient mapping induced by $\mathcal R_\phi$]].
Let $\struct {S / \mathcal R_\phi}, {\circ_{\mathcal R_\phi} }$ be the [[Definition:Quotient Structure|quotient structure defined by $\mathcal R_\phi$]].
Then: 
 :The [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|induced equivalence]] $\mathcal R_\phi$ is a [[Definition:Congruence Relation|congruence relation]] for $\circ$ 
 :There is one and only one [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] $\psi: \struct {S / \mathcal R_\phi}, {\circ_{\mathcal R_\phi} } 	o \struct {T, *}$ which satisfies $\psi  ullet q_{\mathcal R_\phi} = \phi$. 
 where, in order not to cause notational confusion, $ ullet$ is used as the symbol to denote [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composition of mappings]].

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $\struct {T, \oplus}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]].
Let $T^S$ be the [[Definition:Set of All Mappings|set of all mappings]] from $S$ to $T$.
Let $f$ and $g$ be [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphisms]] from $S$ into $T$.
Let $f \oplus' g$ be the [[Definition:Pointwise Operation|pointwise operation on $T^S$ induced by $\oplus$]].
Then $f \oplus' g$ is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ into $\struct {T, \oplus}$.

Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $\left({T, \oplus}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
Let $f$ be a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $S$ into $T$.
Let $f^*$ be the [[Induced Structure Inverse|induced structure inverse of $f$]].
Then $f^*$ is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ into $\left({T, \oplus}ight)$.

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $\struct {T, \oplus}$ be an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
Let $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$ be the [[Definition:Induced Structure|algebraic structure on $T^S$ induced by $\oplus$]].
Then the set of all [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphisms]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ into $\struct {T, \oplus}$ is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$.
solution_steps:
Let $H$ be the set of all [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphisms]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ into $\struct {T, \oplus}$.
=== {{GroupAxiom|0}} ===
From [[Homomorphism on Induced Structure]], we have that $ orall f, g \in H: f \oplus g$ is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ into $\struct {T, \oplus}$.
Hence $\struct {H, \oplus}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
=== {{GroupAxiom|3}} ===
From [[Inverse Mapping in Induced Structure]], if $g$ is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] then its [[Induced Structure Inverse]] $g^*$ is one also.
Thus $g \in H \implies g^* \in H$.
So by the [[Two-Step Subgroup Test]], $\struct {H, \oplus}$ is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $\struct {T^S, \oplus}$.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]].
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
Then there exists [[Definition:Unique|one and only one]] [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\oplus$ such that $f: \left({S, \circ}ight) 	o \left({T, \oplus}ight)$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].
The [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\oplus$ is defined by: 
 :$ orall x, y \in T: x \oplus y = f \left({f^{-1} \left({x}ight) \circ f^{-1} \left({y}ight)}ight)$
The [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\oplus$ is called the '''transplant of $\circ$ under $f$'''.
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nThen there exists a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] from $T$ onto a set $T'$ [[Definition:Disjoint Sets|disjoint]] from $S$.
solution_steps:
Consider the set: 
 :$X = \left\{{y \in S: \left({\exists x \in T: \left({x, y}ight) \in S}ight)}ight\}$
That is, $X$ consists of all the [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $S$ which are the [[Definition:Coordinate|second coordinate]] of some [[Definition:Ordered Pair|ordered pair]] which ''also'' happens to be in $S$ and whose [[Definition:Coordinate|first coordinate]] is in $T$.
From [[Exists Element Not in Set]], we have that $\exists z: z 
otin X$.
Now consider the [[Definition:Cartesian Product|cartesian product]] $T' = T 	imes \left\{{z}ight\}$.
Suppose that $p \in T'$.
Then $p = \left({c, z}ight)$ where $c \in T$.
Suppose $\left({c, z}ight) \in S$.
Then that would mean that $z \in X$.
But we have specifically selected $z$ such that $z 
otin X$.
So $p = \left({c, z}ight) 
otin S$.
Thus we have that $T' \cap S = \varnothing$.
There is an obvious [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] $g: T 	o T'$: 
 :$ orall t \in T: g \left({t}ight) = \left({t, z}ight)$ 
 and hence the result.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let: 
 :$(1): \quad \left({T_2, \oplus_2}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Submagma|submagma]] of $\left({S_2, *_2}\right)$ 
 :$(2): \quad f: \left({T_1, \oplus_1}\right) \to \left({T_2, \oplus_2}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] 
 then there exists: 
 :$(1): \quad$ a [[Definition:Magma|magma]] $\left({S_1, *_1}\right)$ which [[Definition:Submagma|algebraically contains]] $\left({T_1, \oplus_1}\right)$ 
 :$(2): \quad g: \left({S_1, *_1}\right) \to \left({S_2, *_2}\right)$ where $g$ is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] which [[Definition:Extension of Mapping|extends]] $f$. 
 {{Expand|Add the corollary that extends this theorem to structures with two operations.}}
solution_steps:
In order to reduce notational confusion, the [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composition of two mappings]] $f$ and $g$ is denoted $f  ullet g$ instead of the more usual $f \circ g$.
There are two cases to consider: when $T_1$ and $S_2$ are [[Definition:Disjoint Sets|disjoint]], and when they are not.
Suppose $T_1$ and $S_2$ are [[Definition:Disjoint Sets|disjoint]].
Let $S_1$ be the [[Definition:Set|set]] $T_1 \cup \left({S_2 \setminus T_2}ight)$.
Then we can define the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $h: S_2 	o S_1$ as: 
 :$ orall x \in S_2: h \left({x}ight) =  egin{cases} 
 x & : x \in S_2 \setminus T_2

problem:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]].
Let $\left ({C, \circ}ight) \subseteq \left({S, \circ}ight)$ be the [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Let $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Then: 
 :$S \circ' C^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] 
 where $S \circ' C^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Subset Product|subset product]] of $S$ with $C^{-1}$ under $\circ'$ in $T$.

Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]].
Let $\left ({C, \circ}ight) \subseteq \left({S, \circ}ight)$ be the [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] elements of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Let $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Then $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ is its own [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]].
solution_steps:
Let $x \circ' y^{-1}$ be [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ'$, where $x \in S$ and $y \in C$.
We have that $y$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] for $\circ'$.
So by [[Invertible Element of Associative Structure is Cancellable]]: 
 :$y$ is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ'$.
Now by definition of [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse element]]: 
 :$x = \left({x \circ' y^{-1}}ight) \circ' y$
Thus $x$ is also [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ'$.
By [[Cancellable Elements of Semigroup form Subsemigroup]], $x$ is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ$.
So: 
 : $x \in S \implies x \in C$
Thus $x$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] for $\circ'$.
Hence by [[Inverse of Product in Associative Structure]]: 
 : $x \circ' y$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] for $\circ'$.
So every [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable element]] of $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]].
So, by definition, $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ is its own [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e$.
Let $\left ({C, \circ}ight) \subseteq \left({S, \circ}ight)$ be the [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Let $\left({T, \circ'}ight)$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\left({S, \circ}ight)$.
Then $e \in T$ is the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\circ'$.

Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]]\nLet all the [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ be [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].\nThen an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$ is an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]], all of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Let $\struct {T, \circ'}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$.
From [[Inverse Completion is Commutative Monoid]]: 
 :$\struct {T, \circ'} = \struct {S \circ' S^{-1}, \circ'}$ 
 has been shown to be a [[Definition:Commutative Monoid|commutative monoid]].
Taking the [[Definition:Group Axioms|group axioms]] in turn:
=== {{GroupAxiom|0|nolink}} ===
As $\struct {T, \circ'}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]], it is by definition [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== {{GroupAxiom|1|nolink}} ===
As $\struct {T, \circ'}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]], it is by definition [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== {{GroupAxiom|2|nolink}} ===
Let $x \in S$.
Then $x^{-1} \in S^{-1}$ by definition.
As $\struct {T, \circ'} = \struct {S \circ' S^{-1}, \circ'}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]]: 
 :$x \circ' x^{-1} \in T$
This holds for all $x \in S$.
As $\struct {T, \circ'}$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]]: 
 :$\exists e \in T:  orall x \in S: x \circ' x^{-1} = e^{-1} = x \circ' x$
Thus $\struct {T, \circ'}$ has an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity element]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== {{GroupAxiom|3|nolink}} ===
Every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] in $S^{-1}$.
Therefore every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ and $S^{-1}$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]].
From [[Inverse of Product in Associative Structure]], every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S \circ' S^{-1}$ is therefore also [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]].
Thus every element of $T$ has an [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]].
{{qed|lemma}}
All the [[Definition:Group Axioms|group axioms]] are thus seen to be fulfilled, and so $\struct {T, \circ'}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]]. 
 [[Definition:Commutative Operation|Commutativity]] of $\circ$ has been demonstrated.
Hence the result, by definition of [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
{{qed}}

problem: Every [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] containing [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] admits an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]].
solution_steps:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] which has [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]].
From [[Construction of Inverse Completion]], we can construct an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] $\left({T', \oplus'}ight)$ of $\left({S', \oplus'}ight)$, which is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphic]] copy of $S$ under the mapping $\psi: S 	o S'$.
By the [[Embedding Theorem]], there exists a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] $\left({T, \circ}ight)$ which contains $\left({S, \circ}ight)$, and an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] $\Psi$ from $\left({T, \circ}ight)$ to $\left({T', \oplus'}ight)$ which extends $\psi$.
Thus $T$ is an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $S$. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] with [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]]
Let $\struct {C, \circ} \subseteq \struct {S, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of all [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $S$
Let $\struct {S', \circ'}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\struct {S, \circ}$
Let $\phi$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup Homomorphism|(semigroup) homomorphism]] from $\struct {S, \circ}$ into a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] $\struct {T, *}$ such that $\map \phi y$ is [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]] for all $y \in C$.
Then: 
 :$(1): \quad$ There is one and only one [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] $\psi$ from $\struct {S', \circ'}$ into $\struct {T, *}$ [[Definition:Extension of Mapping|extending]] $\phi$ 
 :$(2): \quad  orall x \in S, y \in C: \map \psi {x \circ' y^{-1} } = \map \phi * \paren {\map \phi y}^{-1}$ 
 :$(3): \quad$ If $\phi$ is a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]], then so is $\psi$.

Let the following conditions be fulfilled: 
 : Let $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] with [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] 
 : Let $\phi$ be an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] from $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ into a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] $\left({T, *}\right)$ 
 : Let $\left({S', \circ'}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ 
 : Let $\left({T', \circ'}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\left({T, \circ}\right)$.\nThen there is a unique [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] $\phi': S' \to T'$ extending $\phi$.
solution_steps:
Let $C$ be the [[Definition:Subsemigroup|subsemigroup]] of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $S$.
It is proved that this is a semigroup by [[Cancellable Elements of Semigroup form Subsemigroup]].
The set of [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]] of $T$ is $\phi \left({C}ight)$.
By the [[Extension Theorem for Homomorphisms]], there is: 
 : A unique [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] $\phi'$ from $S'$ into $T'$ extending $\phi$ 
 and: 
 : A unique [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]] $\psi$ from $T'$ into $S'$ extending $\phi^{-1}$.
Therefore, $\psi \circ \phi'$ is an [[Definition:Endomorphism|endomorphism]] of $S'$ whose [[Definition:Restriction of Mapping|restriction]] to $S$ is the [[Definition:Inclusion Mapping|inclusion mapping]] $\iota_S: S 	o S'$: 
 :$ orall x \in S: \iota \left({x}ight) = x$
From [[Inclusion Mapping is Monomorphism]], $\iota_S$ is a [[Definition:Monomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|monomorphism]] from $S$ into $S'$.
But by the [[Extension Theorem for Homomorphisms]], the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity]] [[Definition:Automorphism (Abstract Algebra)|automorphism]] of $S'$ is the only [[Definition:Endomorphism|endomorphism]] of $S'$ extending $\iota_S$ from $S$ into $S'$, so: 
 :$\psi \circ \phi' = I_{S'}$
Similarly: 
 :$\phi' \circ \psi = I_{T'}$
So by [[Bijection iff Left and Right Inverse]], $\phi'$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] and therefore an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: An [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] is unique up to [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]].
solution_steps:
Let $T$ and $T'$ both be [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completions]] of a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] $S$ having [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable elements]].\nThen from the [[Extension Theorem for Isomorphisms]], there is a unique [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] $\phi: T \to T'$ satisfying $\forall x \in S: \phi \left({x}\right) = x$.\nHence the result. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {R, *}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]], all of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].\nLet $\struct {T, *}$ be an [[Definition:Inverse Completion|inverse completion]] of $\struct {R, *}$.\nLet $\circ$ be an [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] on $R$ which [[Definition:Distributive Operation|distributes]] over $*$.\nThen: 
 :$(1): \quad$ There is a unique [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\circ'$ on $T$ which [[Definition:Distributive Operation|distributes]] over $*$ in $T$ and induces on $R$ the [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $\circ$ 
 :$(2): \quad$ If $\circ$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]], then so is $\circ'$ 
 :$(3): \quad$ If $\circ$ is [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]], then so is $\circ'$ 
 :$(4): \quad$ If $e$ is an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\circ$, then $e$ is also an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for $\circ'$ 
 :$(5): \quad$ Every element [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ$ is also [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ'$.
A [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] cannot be [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
solution_steps:
Let $\left({S, \circ}ight)$ be a the [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]].
By [[Definition:Monoid|definition]]: 
 :[[Definition:Identity Element|Identity]]: $\exists e_S \in S:  orall a \in S: a \circ e_S = a = e_S \circ a$
So a monoid must ''at least'' have an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]].
Therefore $e_S \in S$ and so $S$ is not the [[Definition:Empty Set|empty set]].
{{qed}}

problem: A [[Definition:Group|group]] cannot be [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
solution_steps: A [[Definition:Group|group]] is defined as a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]] for which every [[Definition:Element|element]] has an [[Definition:Inverse (Abstract Algebra)|inverse]].\nThus, as a group is already a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]], it must ''at least'' have an [[Definition:Identity (Abstract Algebra)|identity]], therefore can not be empty.\n{{qed}}

problem: Every [[Definition:Group|group]] has [[Definition:Exactly One|exactly one]] [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent element]]: the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]].
solution_steps:
The [[Identity Element is Idempotent]].
From the [[Cancellation Laws]], all [[Definition:Group|group]] [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
The result follows from [[Identity is only Idempotent Cancellable Element]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $x, y \in \struct {G, \circ}$, such that $x$ and $y$ are [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]].\nThen $x$ and $y$ [[Definition:Commute|commute]] {{iff}} $x \circ y$ is also [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]].

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $\struct {\powerset G, \circ_\mathcal P}$ be the [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] consisting of the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $G$ and the [[Definition:Subset Product|operation induced on $\powerset G$ by $\circ$]].\nThen $\struct {\powerset G, \circ_\mathcal P}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $X, Y \subseteq G$.\nThen: 
 :$\paren {X \circ Y}^{-1} = Y^{-1} \circ X^{-1}$ 
 where $X^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse of Subset of Group|inverse]] of $X$.

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $a \in G$ be any [[Definition:Element|element]] of $G$.\nThen the [[Definition:Left Regular Representation|left regular representation]] $\lambda_a$ and the [[Definition:Right Regular Representation|right regular representation]] $\rho_a$ are [[Definition:Permutation|permutations]] of $G$.
solution_steps:
This follows directly from the fact that all [[Definition:Element|elements]] of a [[Definition:Group|group]] are by definition [[Definition:Invertible Element|invertible]].\nTherefore the result [[Regular Representation of Invertible Element is Permutation]] applies. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
If $S$ is a [[Definition:Finite Set|finite]] [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of a [[Definition:Group|group]] $G$, then: 
 :$\card {a \circ S} = \card S = \left|{S \circ a}\right|$\nThat is, $a \circ S$, $S$ and $S \circ a$ are [[Definition:Set Equivalence|equivalent]]: $a \circ S \sim S \sim S \circ a$.
solution_steps: Follows immediately from the fact that both the [[Definition:Left Regular Representation|left]] and [[Definition:Right Regular Representation|right regular representation]] are [[Regular Representations in Group are Permutations|permutations]], and therefore [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]].\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nSuppose that every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $G$ is [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]].\nThen $G$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]].
solution_steps:
Every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $G$ is [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]], that is: 
 :$\forall x \in G: x \circ x = e$\nIn particular, for all $x, y \in G$: 
 :$\paren {x \circ y} \circ \paren {x \circ y} = e$ 
 that is, $x \circ y$ is also [[Definition:Self-Inverse Element|self-inverse]].\nFrom [[Self-Inverse Elements Commute iff Product is Self-Inverse]], it follows that: 
 :$\forall x, y \in G: y \circ x = x \circ y$ 
 that is: $x$ and $y$ [[Definition:Commute|commute]] for all $x, y \in G$.\nHence $G$ is an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nThen $x$ and $y$ [[Definition:Commute|commute]] {{iff}} $x \circ y \circ x^{-1} = y$.

The [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: \left({S, \circ}\right) \to \left({S, \circ}\right)$ on the [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\left({S, \circ}\right)$ is an [[Definition:Automorphism (Abstract Algebra)|automorphism]].\nIts [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] is $S$.
solution_steps:
By definition, an [[Definition:Automorphism (Abstract Algebra)|automorphism]] is an [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]] from an [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] onto itself.
An [[Definition:Isomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|isomorphism]], in turn, is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijective]] [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
From [[Identity Mapping is Bijection]], the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S 	o S$ on the [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] from $S$ onto itself.
Now we need to show it is a [[Definition:Homomorphism (Abstract Algebra)|homomorphism]].
Let $x, y \in S$.

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $\phi: G \to G$ be defined as: 
 :$\forall g \in G: \map \phi g = g \circ g$\nThen $\struct {G, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]] {{iff}} $\phi$ is a [[Definition:Group Endomorphism|(group) endomorphism]].

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
Let $H_1, H_2$ be [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroups]] of $G$.
Let $\phi: H_1 	imes H_2 	o G$ be defined such that: 
 :$ orall 	uple {h_1, h_2} \in H_1 	imes H_2: \map \phi {h_1, h_2} = h_1 \circ h_2$
Then $\phi$ is a [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|homomorphism]] {{iff}} every element of $H_1$ [[Definition:Commute|commutes]] with every element of $H_2$.

Let $\phi: \struct {G, \circ} \to \struct {H, *}$ be a [[Definition:Group Isomorphism|group isomorphism]].\nThen $\struct {G, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]] {{iff}} $\struct {H, *}$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]].
solution_steps:
We have that [[Isomorphism Preserves Commutativity]].
Thus: 
 :$ orall x, y \in G: x \circ y = y \circ x \implies \map \phi x * \map \phi y = \map \phi y * \map \phi x$
Thus if $G$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]], so is $H$.
As $\phi^{-1}: H 	o G$ is [[Inverse of Algebraic Structure Isomorphism is Isomorphism|also an isomorphism]], it is clear that if $H$ is [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]], then so is $G$.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
Let $c \in G$.
We define a new [[Definition:Group Operation|operation]] $*$ on $G$ as: 
 :$ orall x, y \in G: x * y = x \circ c^{-1} \circ y$
Then $\struct {G, *}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]].

Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]] such that $\O \subset S$ (that is, $S$ is [[Definition:Non-Empty Set|non-empty]]).
Let $A * B$ be defined as the [[Definition:Symmetric Difference|symmetric difference]] between $A$ and $B$.
Let $\powerset S$ denote the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$.
Then the [[Definition:Algebraic Structure|algebraic structure]] $\struct {\powerset S, *}$ is an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].
solution_steps:
From [[Power Set is Closed under Symmetric Difference]], we have that $\struct {\powerset S, *}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].\nThe result follows directly from [[Set System Closed under Symmetric Difference is Abelian Group]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nThen: 
 :$\struct {G, \circ} \le \struct {G, \circ}$\nThat is, a [[Definition:Group|group]] is always a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of itself.

Let $G$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]] whose [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] is $e$.\nLet $H$ be a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of [[Definition:Group|group]] $G$.\nThen the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $H$ is also $e$.
solution_steps:
From the [[Definition:Group|definition]], a group is a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]].
Also, [[Cancellation Laws|all of its elements are cancellable]].
The result then follows directly from the result for monoids each of whose [[Definition:Element|elements]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]]: [[Identity of Cancellable Monoid is Identity of Submonoid]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $G$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $H$ be a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$.\nThen for each $h \in H$, the [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $h$ in $H$ is the same as the [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $h$ in $G$.
solution_steps:
Let $h \in H$.
Let: 
 : $h'$ be the [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $h$ in $H$ 
 : $h^{-1}$ be the [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] of $h$ in $G$.
From [[Identity of Subgroup]], $h \circ h' = e$.
From [[Inverse in Group is Unique]], it follows that $h' = h^{-1}$.
{{qed}}

problem: A [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]] is itself [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]].
solution_steps: Follows directly from [[Restriction of Commutative Operation is Commutative]].\n{{qed}}

problem:
The [[Definition:Set Intersection|intersection]] of two [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroups]] of a [[Definition:Group|group]] is itself a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of that [[Definition:Group|group]]: 
 :$\forall H_1, H_2 \le \struct {G, \circ}: H_1 \cap H_2 \le G$\nIt also follows that $H_1 \cap H_2 \le H_1$ and $H_1 \cap H_2 \le H_2$.

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].
Let $H, K \le G$ be [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroups]] of $G$.
Let neither $H \subseteq K$ nor $K \subseteq H$.
Then $H \cup K$ is ''not'' a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$.
solution_steps:
As neither $H \subseteq K$ nor $K \subseteq H$, it follows from [[Set Difference with Superset is Empty Set]] that neither $H \setminus K = \O$ nor $K \setminus H = \O$.
So, let $h \in H \setminus K, k \in K \setminus H$.
Thus, $h 
otin K, k 
otin H$.
If $\struct {H \cup K, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]], then it must be [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
If $\struct {H \cup K, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]], then $h \circ k \in H \cup K \implies h \circ k \in H \lor h \circ k \in K$.
If $h \circ k \in H$ then $h^{-1} \circ h \circ k \in H \implies k \in H$.
If $h \circ k \in K$ then $h \circ k \circ k^{-1} \in K \implies h \in K$.
So $h \circ k$ can be in neither $H$ nor $K$.
Therefore $\struct {H \cup K, \circ}$ is not [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]].
Therefore $H \cup K$ is not a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ and $\struct {H, *}$ be [[Definition:Group|groups]].\nLet $f: G \to H$ and $g: G \to H$ be [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphisms]].\nThen the [[Definition:Set|set]]: 
 :$S = \set {x \in G: \map f x = \map g x}$ 
 is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$.
solution_steps:
Let the [[Definition:Identity Element|identities]] of $\struct {G, \circ}$ and $\struct {H, *}$ be $e_G$ and $e_H$ respectively.
By [[Homomorphism to Group Preserves Identity]]: 
 :$\map f {e_G} = \map g {e_G} = e_H$
Thus $e_G \in S$, and so $S 
e \O$.
Similarly, from [[Homomorphism to Group Preserves Inverses]], $x \in S \implies x^{-1} \in S$.
Let $x, y \in S$.

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nThen: 
 : $\forall H \le \struct {G, \circ}: H \circ H = H$
solution_steps:
From [[Magma Subset Product with Self]], we have: 
 :$H \circ H \subseteq H$\nLet $e$ be the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $G$.\nBy [[Identity of Subgroup]], it is also the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $H$.\nSo: 
 {{begin-eqn}} 
 {{eqn | l = h 
 | o = \in 
 | r = H 
 | c = 
 }}\n{{eqn | ll= \leadsto 
 | l = e \circ h 
 | o = \in 
 | r = H \circ H 
 | c = 
 }}\n{{eqn | ll= \leadsto 
 | l = h 
 | o = \in 
 | r = H \circ H 
 | c = 
 }}\n{{eqn | ll= \leadsto 
 | l = H 
 | o = \subseteq 
 | r = H \circ H 
 | c = 
 }} 
 {{end-eqn}}\nHence the result from the [[Definition:Set Equality|definition of set equality]].\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Group|group]].\nLet $H$ be a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$.\nThen: 
 :$H^{-1} = H$ 
 where $H^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse of Subset of Group|inverse]] of $H$.
solution_steps:
As $H$ is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $G$: 
 :$\forall h \in H: h^{-1} \in H$\nThe result follows.

problem:
Let $\struct {G_1, \circ}$ and $\struct {G_2, *}$ be [[Definition:Group|groups]].\nLet $\phi: \struct {G_1, \circ} \to \struct {G_2, *}$ be a [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphism]]. \nThen: 
 :$H \le G_1 \implies \phi \sqbrk H \le G_2$ 
 where: 
 :$\phi \sqbrk H$ denotes the [[Definition:Image of Subset under Mapping|image of $H$ under $\phi$]] 
 :$\le$ denotes [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]].\nThat is, [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphism]] preserves [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroups]].
solution_steps:
Let $H \le G_1$.
First note that from [[Null Relation is Mapping iff Domain is Empty Set]]: 
 :$H 
e \O \implies \phi \sqbrk H 
e \O$ 
 and so $\phi \sqbrk H$ is not [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
Next, let $x, y \in \phi \sqbrk H$.
Then: 
 :$\exists h_1, h_2 \in H: x = \map \phi {h_1}, y = \map \phi {h_2}$
From the definition of [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|Group Homomorphism]], we have: 
 :$\map \phi {h_1^{-1} \circ h_2} = x^{-1} * y$
Since $H$ is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]]: 
 :$h_1^{-1} \circ h_2 \in H$
Hence: 
 :$x^{-1} * y \in \phi \sqbrk H$
Thus from the [[One-Step Subgroup Test]]: 
 :$\phi \sqbrk H \le G_2$
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\phi: G_1 \to G_2$ be a [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphism]].\nThen: 
 :$\Img \phi \le G_2$ 
 where $\le$ denotes the relation of being a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]].
solution_steps:
This is a special case of [[Group Homomorphism Preserves Subgroups]], where we set $H = G_1$. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: [[Definition:Conjugate of Group Element|Conjugacy of group elements]] is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]].

The [[Definition:Kernel of Group Homomorphism|kernel of a group homomorphism]] is a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of its [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]]: 
 :$\map \ker \phi \le \Dom \phi$
A [[Definition:Commutative Semigroup|commutative semigroup]] is an [[Definition:Entropic Structure|entropic structure]].

Let $\struct {G, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]].\nLet the operation $*$ be defined on $G$ such that: 
 :$\forall x, y \in G: x * y = x \circ y^{-1}$\nThen $\struct {G, *}$ is an [[Definition:Entropic Structure|entropic structure]].

Let $\struct {S, *, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Cancellable Semiring|cancellable semiring]] with [[Definition:Unity of Semiring|unity]] $1_S$.\nThen the [[Definition:Distributand|distributand]] $*$ is [[Definition:Commutative Operation|commutative]].\nThat is to say, $\struct {S, *, \circ}$ is also an [[Definition:Additive Semiring|additive semiring]].
A [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] cannot be [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
solution_steps:
In a ring $\struct {R, +, \circ}$, $\struct {R, +}$ forms a [[Definition:Group|group]].
From [[Group is not Empty]], the [[Definition:Group|group]] $\struct {R, +}$ contains at least the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]], so cannot be [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
So every ring $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ contains at least the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for [[Definition:Ring Addition|ring addition]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] whose [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] is $0_R$.\nThen: 
 :$\forall x \in R: 0_R \circ x = 0_R = x \circ 0_R$\nThat is, the [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] is a [[Definition:Zero Element|zero element]] for the [[Definition:Ring Product|ring product]], thereby justifying its name.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall x, y \in \struct {R, +, \circ}: \paren {-x} \circ y = -\paren {x \circ y} = x \circ \paren {-y}$ 
 where $\paren {-x}$ denotes the [[Definition:Ring Negative|negative]] of $x$.
A [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] can have no more than one [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]].
solution_steps:
Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].
If $\struct {R, \circ}$ has an [[Definition:Identity (Abstract Algebra)|identity]], then it is a [[Definition:Monoid|monoid]].
From [[Identity of Monoid is Unique]], it follows that such an identity is unique.
{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Null Ring|null ring]] is the only [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] in which the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] and [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] coincide.
solution_steps:
The single element of the [[Definition:Null Ring|null ring]] serves as an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] for both of the operations.
So, in this particular ring, the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] and the [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] are the same [[Definition:Element|element]].
A [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null ring]] contains some non-zero element $a$.
Since $1_R \circ a = a 
e 0_R = 0_R \circ a$, then $1_R 
e 0_R$.
So if a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] is [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null]], its [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] cannot be [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]].
So a ring is [[Definition:Null Ring|null]] {{iff}} its [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] is also its [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]].
{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] in a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] is a [[Definition:Unit of Ring|unit]].
solution_steps:
Let $\left({R, +, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]] $1_R$.\nFrom [[Identity is Self-Inverse]]: 
 :$1_R^{-1} = 1_R \in R \implies 1_R \in U_R$\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]].\nThen: 
 :$\struct {\set {1_R, -1_R}, \circ} \le U_R$\nThat is, the [[Definition:Set|set]] consisting of the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] and its [[Definition:Ring Negative|negative]] forms a [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]].

Let $\left({R, +, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]].\nLet $z \in U_R$, where $U_R$ is the [[Definition:Set|set]] of [[Definition:Unit of Ring|units]].\nThen: 
 :$\left({- z}\right)^{-1} = - \left({z^{-1}}\right)$. 
 where $z^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Ring Product Inverse|ring product inverse]] of $z$.

Let $\left({R, +, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]].\nLet $z \in U_R$, where $U_R$ is the [[Definition:Set|set]] of [[Definition:Unit of Ring|units]].\nThen: 
 : $(1): \quad \forall x \in R: -\left({x \circ z^{-1}}\right) = \left({- x}\right) \circ z^{-1} = x \circ \left({\left({- z}\right)^{-1}}\right)$ 
 : $(2): \quad \forall x \in R: -\left({z^{-1} \circ x}\right) = z^{-1} \circ \left({- x}\right) = \left({\left({- z}\right)^{-1}}\right) \circ x$

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative and Unitary Ring|commutative ring with unity]].\nLet $\struct {U_R, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.\nThen: 
 :$\displaystyle \forall x \in R: -\frac x z = \frac {-x} z = \frac x {-z}$ 
 where $\dfrac x z$ is defined as $x \circ \paren {z^{-1} }$, that is the [[Definition:Division Product|division product]] of $x$ by $z$.
solution_steps: Follows directly from [[Product of Negative with Product Inverse]] and the definition of [[Definition:Division Product|division product]].\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative and Unitary Ring|commutative ring with unity]].
Let $\struct {U_R, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Let $a, c \in R, b, d \in U_R$.
Then: 
 :$\dfrac a b + \dfrac c d = \dfrac {a \circ d + b \circ c} {b \circ d}$ 
 where $\dfrac x z$ is defined as $x \circ \paren {z^{-1} }$, that is, $x$ [[Definition:Division Product|divided by]] $z$.
The [[Definition:Binary Operation|operation]] $+$ is [[Definition:Well-Defined Operation|well-defined]].
That is: 
 :$\dfrac a b = \dfrac {a'} {b'}, \dfrac c d = \dfrac {c'} {d'} \implies \dfrac a b + \dfrac c d = \dfrac {a'} {b'} + \dfrac {c'} {d'}$ 
 {{questionable|This is an existing operation and thus already well-defined. See talk.}}

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative and Unitary Ring|commutative ring with unity]].
Let $\struct {U_R, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Let $a, b \in R, c, d \in U_R$.
Then: 
 :$\dfrac a c = \dfrac b d \iff a \circ d = b \circ c$ 
 where $\dfrac x z$ is defined as $x \circ \paren {z^{-1} }$, that is, $x$ [[Definition:Division Product|divided by]] $z$.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative and Unitary Ring|commutative ring with unity]].
Let $\struct {U_R, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Let $a, b \in R, c, d \in U_R$.
Then: 
 :$\dfrac a c \circ \dfrac b d = \dfrac {a \circ b} {c \circ d}$ 
 where $\dfrac x z$ is defined as $x \circ \paren {z^{-1} }$, that is, $x$ [[Definition:Division Product|divided by]] $z$.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Commutative and Unitary Ring|commutative ring with unity]].
Let $\struct {U_R, \circ}$ be the [[Definition:Group of Units of Ring|group of units]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Let $a, b \in U_R$.
Then: 
 :$\paren {\dfrac a b}^{-1} = \dfrac {1_R} {\paren {a / b}} = \dfrac b a$ 
 where $\dfrac x z$ is defined as $x \circ \paren {z^{-1} }$, that is, $x$ [[Definition:Division Product|divided by]] $z$.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].
Let $x \in R$ be a [[Definition:Proper Zero Divisor|proper zero divisor]] of $R$.
Then: 
 :$\paren {x \divides 0_R} \land \paren {x \circ y = 0_R} \land \paren {y 
e 0_R} \implies y \divides 0_R$
That is, if $x$ is a [[Definition:Proper Zero Divisor|proper zero divisor]], then whatever non-[[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] [[Definition:Element|element]] you form the product with it by to get [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] must itself be a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]].
solution_steps:
Follows directly from the definition of [[Definition:Proper Zero Divisor|proper zero divisor]].
If $y 
e 0_R$ and $x \circ y = 0_R$ and $x \in R^*$ (which is has to be if it's a proper zero divisor), then all the criteria of being a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]] are fulfilled by $y$.
{{qed}}

problem: A [[Definition:Unit of Ring|unit of a ring]] is not a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]].
The [[Definition:Ring Product|ring product]] of a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]] with any ring element is a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]].

If the [[Definition:Ring Product|ring product]] of two [[Definition:Element|elements]] of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] is a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]], then one of the two elements must be a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]].

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] which is [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|not null]].\nLet $z \in R^*$.\nThen $z$ is a [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisor]] {{iff}} $z$ is not [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]] for $\circ$.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null]] [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].\nThen $R$ has no [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisors]] {{iff}} $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
solution_steps:
=== Necessary Condition ===
Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null]] [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] with no [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisors]].
The set $R^* = R \setminus \set {0_R} 
e \O$ as $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null]].
All elements of $R$ are not [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisors]], and therefore are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]]. 
 $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ$, from the fact that there are no [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisors]], and also that $\struct {R, \circ}$ is also closed.
From [[Restriction of Associative Operation is Associative]], [[Definition:Ring Product|ring product]] is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] on $\struct {R^*, +, \circ}$, as it is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] on $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Thus $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Let $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]].
Then: 
 :$
eg \exists x, y \in R^*: x \circ y 
otin R^*$
Thus: 
 :$
eg \exists x, y \in R^*: x \circ y = 0_R$ 
 and the result follows.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\left({R, +, \circ}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Non-Null Ring|non-null]] [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] with no [[Definition:Proper Zero Divisor|(proper) zero divisors]].
Let $x \in R$.
Then: 
 :$x \circ x = x \iff x \in \left\{{0_R, 1_R}ight\}$
That is, the only [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $R$ that are [[Definition:Idempotent Element|idempotent]] are [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] and [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]].
solution_steps:
We have $0_R \circ 0_R = 0_R$, so that sorts out one [[Definition:Element|element]].
Let $R^*$ be the [[Definition:Ring Less Zero|ring $R$ without the zero]]: $R^* = R \setminus \left\{{0_R}ight\}$.
By [[Ring Element is Zero Divisor iff not Cancellable]], all elements of $R^*$ that are not [[Definition:Zero Divisor|zero divisors]] are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Therefore all elements of $R^*$ are [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]].
Suppose $x \circ x = x$ where $x 
e 0_R$.
Then $x \circ x = x = x \circ 1_R$.
As $x$ is [[Definition:Cancellable Element|cancellable]], the result follows. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Division Ring|division ring]].\nThen $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]].
solution_steps:
A [[Definition:Division Ring|division ring]] by definition is a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]], and therefore not [[Definition:Null Ring|null]].\nA [[Definition:Division Ring|division ring]] by definition has no [[Definition:Zero Divisor of Ring|zero divisors]], so $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Ring Less Zero is Semigroup for Product iff No Proper Zero Divisors|semigroup]]. 
 $1_R \in \struct {R^*, \circ}$ and so the [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] of $\circ$ is in $\struct {R^*, \circ}$.\nBy the definition of a [[Definition:Division Ring|division ring]], each element of $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Unit of Ring|unit]], and therefore has a unique [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverse]] in $\struct {R^*, \circ}$.\nThus $\struct {R^*, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] with an [[Definition:Identity Element|identity]] and [[Definition:Inverse Element|inverses]] and so is a [[Definition:Group|group]].\n{{qed}} 
 [[Category:Division Rings]] 
 [[Category:Group Theory]]

problem:
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.\nThen $\struct {S, +, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ {{iff}} these all hold: 
 :$(1): \quad S \ne \O$ 
 :$(2): \quad \forall x, y \in S: x + \paren {-y} \in S$ 
 :$(3): \quad \forall x, y \in S: x \circ y \in S$
solution_steps:
=== Necessary Condition ===
If $S$ is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$, the conditions hold by virtue of the [[Definition:Ring Axioms|ring axioms]] as applied to $S$.
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Conversely, suppose the conditions hold.

We check that the [[Definition:Ring Axioms|ring axioms]] hold for $S$. 
 :$(1): \quad$ '''{{Ring-axiom|A}}:''' By $1$ and $2$ above, it follows from: 
 :::: the [[One-Step Subgroup Test]] 
 ::::[[Subgroup of Abelian Group is Abelian]] 
 :::that $\struct {S, +}$ is an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian]] [[Definition:Subgroup|subgroup]] of $\struct {R, +}$, and therefore an [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian group]]. 
 :$(2): \quad$ '''{{Ring-axiom|M0}}:''' From $3$, $\struct {S, \circ}$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]]. 
 :$(3): \quad$ '''{{Ring-axiom|M1}}:''' From [[Restriction of Associative Operation is Associative]], $\circ$ is [[Definition:Associative Operation|associative]] on $R$, therefore also associative on $S$. 
 :$(4): \quad$ '''{{Ring-axiom|D}}:''' From [[Restriction of Operation Distributivity]], $\circ$ [[Definition:Distributive Operation|distributes]] over $+$ for the whole of $R$, therefore for $S$ also.\nSo $\struct {S, +, \circ}$ is a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]], and therefore a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.\nThen $\struct {S, +\restriction_S, \circ \restriction_S}$ is a [[Definition:Subdomain|subdomain]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ {{iff}} these conditions hold: 
 :$(1): \quad$ $\struct {S, + \restriction_S, \circ \restriction_S}$ is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ 
 :$(2): \quad$ The [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] of $R$ is also in $S$, that is $1_R = 1_S$.
solution_steps: By [[Idempotent Elements of Ring with No Proper Zero Divisors]], it follows that the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] of a [[Definition:Subdomain|subdomain]] is the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] of the [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] it's a [[Definition:Subdomain|subdomain]] of.\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] $\struct {R, +, \circ}$\nThen $\map {C_R} S$, the [[Definition:Centralizer of Ring Subset|centralizer]] of $S$ in $R$, is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $R$.
The [[Definition:Center of Ring|center]] $\map Z R$ of a ring $R$ is a [[Definition:Commutative Ring|commutative]] [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $R$.
solution_steps: Follows directly from the definition of [[Definition:Center of Ring|center]] and [[Centralizer of Ring Subset is Subring]].\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({R, +, \circ}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]], and let $J$ be an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $R$.\nThen $J$ is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $R$.
solution_steps: This follows directly from the definition of an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] and [[Subring Test]].\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].\nThen $R$ is an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $R$.

Let $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ be a [[Definition:Ring with Unity|ring with unity]].\nLet $J$ be an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $R$.\nIf $J$ contains a [[Definition:Unit of Ring|unit]] of $R$, then $J = R$.

Let $J$ be a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.\nThen $J$ is an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$ {{iff}} these all hold: 
 : $(1): \quad J \ne \O$ 
 : $(2): \quad \forall x, y \in J: x + \paren {-y} \in J$ 
 : $(3): \quad \forall j \in J, r \in R: r \circ j \in J, j \circ r \in J$
solution_steps:
=== Necessary Condition ===
Let $J$ be an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
Then conditions $(1)$ to $(3)$ hold by virtue of the [[Definition:Ring Axioms|ring axioms]] and $J$ being an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Suppose conditions $(1)$ to $(3)$ hold.
As $r \in J \implies r \in R$, if $(3)$ holds for $J$, then $J$ is [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed]] under $\circ$ and condition $(3)$ of [[Subring Test]] holds.
Thus, $J$ is a [[Definition:Subring|subring]] of $R$.
As $(3)$ defines the condition for $J$, being a [[Definition:Subring|subring]], to be an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]], the result holds.
So $J$ is an [[Definition:Ideal of Ring|ideal]] of $\struct {R, +, \circ}$.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\struct {R_1, +_1, \circ_1}$ be a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]], and $\struct {R_2, +_2, \circ_2}$ be a [[Definition:Closed Algebraic Structure|closed algebraic structure]].\nLet $\phi: R_1 \to R_2$ be an [[Definition:Epimorphism (Abstract Algebra)|epimorphism]].\nThen $\struct {R_2, +_2, \circ_2}$ is a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].
solution_steps:
From [[Epimorphism Preserves Groups]], we have that if $\struct {R_1, +_1}$ is a [[Definition:Group|group]] then so is $\struct {R_2, +_2}$.
From [[Epimorphism Preserves Semigroups]], we have that if $\struct {R_1, \circ_1}$ is a [[Definition:Semigroup|semigroup]] then so is $\struct {R_2, \circ_2}$.
From [[Epimorphism Preserves Distributivity]], we have that if $\circ_1$ distributes over $+_1$ then $\circ_2$ distributes over $+_2$.
So it follows from the definition of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] that if $\struct {R_1, +_1, \circ_1}$ is a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]] then so is $\struct {R_2, +_2, \circ_2}$.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\phi: \left({R_1, +_1, \circ_1}\right) \to \left({R_2, +_2, \circ_2}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Ring Homomorphism|ring homomorphism]].\nThen $\phi: \left({R_1, +_1}\right) \to \left({R_2, +_2}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphism]].
solution_steps:
From the definition of a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]], both $\left({R_1, +_1}\right)$ and $\left({R_2, +_2}\right)$ are [[Definition:Abelian Group|abelian groups]].\nThe result follows from the definitions of [[Definition:Ring Homomorphism|ring homomorphism]] and [[Definition:Group Homomorphism|group homomorphism]]. 
 {{Qed}} 
 [[Category:Ring Homomorphisms]] 
 [[Category:Group Homomorphisms]]

problem:
Let $\struct {D, +, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] whose [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]] is $0_D$.\nThen every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $D$ is a [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]] of [[Definition:Ring Zero|zero]]: 
 :$\forall x \in D: x \divides 0_D$
solution_steps:
By definition, an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] is a [[Definition:Ring (Abstract Algebra)|ring]].
So, from [[Ring Product with Zero]]: 
 :$ orall x \in D: 0_D = x \circ 0_D$.
The result follows from the definition of [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {D, +, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] whose [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] is $1_D$.\nThen [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] is a [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]] of every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $D$: 
 :$\forall x \in D: 1_D \divides x$\nAlso: 
 :$\forall x \in D: -1_D \divides x$
solution_steps:
The element $1_D$ is the [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] of $\struct {D, +, \circ}$, and so: 
 :$1_D \in D: x = 1_D \circ x$
Similarly, from [[Product of Ring Negatives]]: 
 :$-1_D \in D: x = \paren {-1_D} \circ \paren {-x}$
The result follows from the definition of [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {D, +, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]] whose [[Definition:Unity of Ring|unity]] is $1_D$.\nThen every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $D$ is a [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]] of itself: 
 :$\forall x \in D: x \divides x$
solution_steps:
Follows directly from the definition of [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]]: 
 :$\forall x \in D: \exists 1_D \in D: x = 1_D \circ x$\n{{qed}} 
 [[Category:Integral Domains]]

problem:
Let $\struct {D, +, \circ}$ be an [[Definition:Integral Domain|integral domain]].\nLet $x, y, z \in D$.\nThen: 
 :$x \divides y \land y \divides z \implies x \divides z$
solution_steps:
Let $x \divides y \land y \divides z$.\nThen from the definition of [[Definition:Divisor of Ring Element|divisor]], we have: 
 :$x \divides y \iff \exists s \in D: y = s \circ x$ 
 :$y \divides z \iff \exists t \in D: z = t \circ y$\nThen: 
 :$z = t \circ \paren {s \circ x} = \paren {t \circ s} \circ x$\nThus: 
 :$\exists \paren {t \circ s} \in D: z = \paren {t \circ s} \circ x$ 
 and the result follows. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: The [[Integers form Integral Domain|integers]] $\struct {\Z, +, \times}$ form a [[Definition:Unique Factorization Domain|unique factorization domain]].
solution_steps:
Follows directly from: 
 :$(1) \quad$ the [[Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic|fundamental theorem of arithmetic]] 
 :$(2) \quad$ the fact that [[Integers form Integral Domain|$\struct {\Z, +, \times}$ is an integral domain]] 
 :$(3) \quad$ the definitions [[Definition:Complete Factorization|complete factorization]] and [[Definition:Equivalent Factorizations|equivalent factorizations]].\n{{qed}}
